title: Agents Meet the Real World
subtitle: Real APIs, error handling, memory — take your agents from simulated toys to production-ready tools
heroTitle: Agents Meet the Real World

objectives.title: Connect Agents to Real APIs
objectives.description: Replace simulated tools with live data sources. Build a Wikipedia search tool and a web page fetcher that work with real HTTP requests.

objectives.title: Handle Real-World Failures
objectives.description: Build agents that gracefully handle 404s, timeouts, and bad input — instead of crashing, they adapt and try alternatives.

objectives.title: Give Agents Memory
objectives.description: Implement conversation history so your agent remembers context across turns. Understand why memory is just a list — and why that matters.

objectives.title: Iterate Your Session 4 Product
objectives.description: Apply real tools, error handling, and memory concepts to upgrade the product and agent you built last session.

agenda.title: Part 1: NanoClaw Live Demo
agenda.description: See a production AI agent in action — the theory from Session 4 running in the real world.
bulletPoints: LIVE DEMO: Send a WhatsApp message to NanoClaw, watch it reason and respond in real-time
Code highlights: How index.ts, ipc-mcp.ts, and db.ts work together (Session 4 overflow)
Key observation: Real agents deal with real problems — network delays, bad input, context limits
The gap: Your Session 4 agent used fake data. Today we close that gap.

agenda.title: Part 2: From Fake to Real
agenda.description: Why simulated tools aren't enough, and how to connect agents to the real world.
bulletPoints: The problem: Your Session 4 stock tool returns the SAME price every time. Real data changes.
Real APIs 101: What is an API endpoint? HTTP requests. JSON responses. Status codes.
Wikipedia API: Free, no key needed, returns structured summaries — perfect first real tool
Web fetching: requests + BeautifulSoup = read any web page programmatically
ACTION: Open Colab now, run Setup cells while I talk

agenda.title: Part 3: Hands-On — Real-World Agent
agenda.description: Build an agent with real tools that fetches live data from the internet.
bulletPoints: 5:35-5:45 — Bring back the calculator from Session 4 + the run_agent() loop as foundation
5:45-5:55 — Build Wikipedia search tool: live API, real data, error handling for 404s
5:55-6:10 — Build web page fetcher: requests + BeautifulSoup, timeout handling, text truncation
6:10-6:20 — Multi-tool challenge: Ask questions that require calculator + Wikipedia + web fetch together

agenda.title: Break
agenda.description: Stretch, grab coffee. Your agent now talks to the real internet.

agenda.title: Part 4: Agent Memory
agenda.description: The missing piece — agents that remember what you said 5 minutes ago.
bulletPoints: THE PROBLEM: Ask 'Who founded Tesla?' then 'How old is he?' — agent forgets everything
The fix: Conversation history = just a list of messages. That's it. Memory demystified.
LIVE CODING: run_agent_with_memory() — maintains context across turns
Multi-turn test: research → follow-up → calculation chain
Trade-offs: More memory = more tokens = more cost. When to forget on purpose.

agenda.title: Part 5: Product Iteration Workshop
agenda.description: Apply what you learned to upgrade your Session 4 product.
bulletPoints: Review: Session 4 = simulated tools. Session 5 = real APIs + errors + memory.
Exercise: Pick one real API that would make your Session 4 product actually useful
Design: What errors could happen? How should your agent handle them?
Memory: Does your product need multi-turn conversation? Why or why not?
Share: 60-second pitch of your upgraded product concept to a neighbor

agenda.title: Part 6: Wrap-Up + Homework
agenda.description: From consumer to builder to architect — and the journey ahead.
bulletPoints: The arc: Session 4 built agents with fake tools → Session 5 connected them to the real world
You learned 3 production essentials: real APIs, error handling, conversation memory
Next session: Agent Patterns & Production Intelligence — ReAct reasoning, multi-agent architectures, and MCP. The patterns that power NanoClaw.
Homework preview: Add a real API tool to your agent, test edge cases, design memory strategy

resources.description: Build an agent with real APIs: Wikipedia search, web page fetcher, error handling, and conversation memory. Uses Claude/Anthropic SDK.

resources.title: Real-World Agent Notebook (Gemini)
resources.description: Same real-world agent workshop using Google Gemini. Identical tools and structure — different LLM backend. Requires a Google API key.

resources.title: Agentic AI Deep-Dive
resources.description: Visual explainer from Session 4: Chatbot vs Agent, the Agent Loop, and the NanoClaw case study. Review before diving into real APIs.

resources.title: Wikipedia REST API Docs
resources.description: Free, no-key API for page summaries, search, and more. The real API your agent uses in today's workshop.

resources.title: Beautiful Soup Documentation
resources.description: Python library for parsing HTML and extracting text from web pages. Powers the web fetcher tool in today's notebook.

resources.description: Official documentation for giving Claude tools. The foundation of building AI agents with the Anthropic API.

resources.title: Google Colab
resources.description: Free cloud-based Python notebooks. No installation needed — just open and start coding.

activities.title: Real-World Agent Workshop
activities.description: Build an agent that connects to live APIs — real data, real errors, real power:

steps.title: Bring Back the Foundation (10 min)
steps.description: Copy the calculator tool and run_agent() loop from Session 4. Verify everything works before adding new tools.

steps.title: Wikipedia Search Tool (10 min)
steps.description: Build a tool that queries the Wikipedia API for any topic. Handle 404s for pages that don't exist. Test with 'Suffolk University' and 'artificial intelligence'.

steps.title: Web Page Fetcher (15 min)
steps.description: Build a tool that fetches any URL, extracts text with BeautifulSoup, and truncates to 3000 chars. Handle timeouts and connection errors.

steps.title: Multi-Tool Challenge (10 min)
steps.description: Ask questions that need all 3 tools: 'Look up Suffolk University on Wikipedia, then visit their website and calculate how many years they've been open.'

activities.title: Agent Memory Lab
activities.description: Give your agent the ability to remember — and understand why it matters:

steps.title: See the Problem (3 min)
steps.description: Ask 'Who founded Tesla?' then 'How old is he?' — watch the agent fail because it has no memory of the first question.

steps.title: Build Memory (7 min)
steps.description: Implement run_agent_with_memory() that maintains a conversation history list. Memory is just a list — that's the key insight.

steps.title: Multi-Turn Test (5 min)
steps.description: Chain 3+ questions: research a topic → ask follow-up → request a calculation based on previous answers.

steps.title: Memory Trade-offs (5 min)
steps.description: Discuss: More memory = more tokens = more cost. When should an agent forget? How does NanoClaw handle this?

codeBlock.code:
def run_agent_with_memory(user_message, tools, tool_functions,
                         conversation_history=None):
    """
    Agent with memory! Maintains conversation history
    across multiple turns.
    """
    if conversation_history is None:
        conversation_history = []

    # Add new user message to history
    conversation_history.append(
        {"role": "user", "content": user_message}
    )

    # Send FULL history to LLM (that's the memory!)
    # ... same agent loop, but with conversation_history
    # instead of a single message

    return final_answer, conversation_history

activities.title: Product Iteration Workshop
activities.description: Apply real-world concepts to upgrade your Session 4 product:

steps.title: Pick a Real API (5 min)
steps.description: What real data source would make your product actually useful? Weather, news, maps, stocks, recipes? Find one that's free and doesn't need a key.

steps.title: Design Error Handling (5 min)
steps.description: List 3 things that could go wrong with your real API. How should your agent respond to each failure? Write the fallback messages.

steps.title: Memory Strategy (5 min)
steps.description: Does your product need multi-turn conversation? What should it remember? What should it forget? Write a 3-sentence memory strategy.

steps.title: 60-Second Pitch (5 min)
steps.description: Tell a neighbor: 'My product now uses [real API] to [do what]. It handles [these errors] and remembers [this context].'

takeaways.text: Real APIs change everything. Simulated tools are training wheels. Today you connected your agent to Wikipedia and the live web — real data, real value.
Errors are features, not bugs. Production agents don't crash on 404s — they adapt. You built agents that handle timeouts, missing pages, and bad input gracefully.
Memory is just a list. Conversation history isn't magic — it's appending messages to a list and sending the full list each time. Simple concept, massive impact.
Session 4 → Session 5 → Session 6. Fake tools → real APIs → production patterns. Each session builds on the last. Next session adds ReAct reasoning, multi-agent, and MCP.
The business decision is which APIs to connect. The agent loop is ~50 lines. The value is in choosing the right data sources and designing how your agent handles the messy real world.

homework.label: Build:
homework.text: Add a new real API tool to your notebook (weather, news, recipes, etc.). It must be a live API — no simulated data. Test it with 3 different queries.

homework.label: Break:
homework.text: Deliberately trigger 3 different error conditions in your agent (bad URL, nonexistent page, timeout). Document how your agent handles each one.

homework.label: Remember:
homework.text: Use run_agent_with_memory() to have a 5-turn conversation with your agent. Save the conversation and annotate where memory helped vs where it wasn't needed.

homework.label: Design:
homework.text: Write a 1-page upgrade plan for your Session 4 product: which real APIs to add, what errors to handle, and your memory strategy.

nextSession.title: Session 6: Agent Patterns & Production Intelligence
nextSession.description: ReAct reasoning, multi-agent architectures, and the patterns that separate demos from production systems.
